story:
The Galactic Bracket was built to make humanity the punchline: twenty tiers, fight to the finish, no mercy—every alien bristling with acid glands, spinning blades, and armor. Humans were invited as a novelty, allowed to carry “tools.” Then one stubborn competitor arrived with a bow, a potato gun, an axe, a battered shield—and a belly full of beans. He smirked like he’d trained his cheeks for this moment.

Round one: the Snipod, a mucus-spitting inchworm on wheels with fire breath. Odds were twenty to one against the human. Howard Cossmell, legendary announcer, rasped play-by-play as the man panicked, lifted his shield, and let loose a thunderclap of gas that rolled like swamp fog. Suction cups dropped; the Snipod gagged, then folded. “Humanity advances!” Howard cried, nasal and reverent, while co-commentator Jerry Gaxx gagged into the desk. A few brave fans pinched their noses and chanted, “Praise the beans!” The smell washed the dome like a storm and settled like fresh, terrible rain.

Round two: the Tyladicyte, a skyscraper of spinning saws. Blades flew; the human dodged, snatched one midair, flung it back—and followed with another seismic blast. Discs stuttered. The monster choked. Jerry wheezed, “My teeth taste like cabbage,” but Howard only whispered, “Destiny.” Vendors waved cans of beans. Kids sang, “All hail beans, the sacred fruit!” Referees pinched their noses and muttered about “protocol.” Officials reviewed the rulebook and, with grim faces, confirmed it: under the open-equipment clause, anything a competitor could carry or produce that wasn’t an external bioweapon was permitted. Personal emissions weren’t prohibited. Legal.

By the semifinals against the Elighastra, Howard’s awe warred with suffocation. The human’s fart squeaked and trumpeted; the creature staggered. The axe came down. The crowd split—half pilgrims, half scramblers for the exits—as a clumsy hymn rose: “Blessed be the fart, amen! Beans unite us once again!” Jerry called it sewage; Howard called it art.

The final brought a centipede tank bristling with razors and venom sacs. The bookies stopped posting odds. The arena fell silent. The human braced, abdomen tight. The blast detonated. Rafters shook. Lights flickered. The centipede vomited neon bile and reeled. Jerry screamed, “I can taste it in every swallow!” Howard forced one last adoring line through coughs—then his mic clattered. He collapsed in the booth. Medics came running, but the broadcast faded to static. Later, the league added emergency ventilation and masks; debate flared about “biological warfare,” yet within the established rules, the win stood.

When the bell rang, humanity’s unlikely champion was crowned: the Butt Blaster. The crowd mourned Howard. Crews welded a statue of the Butt Blaster from melted nacho trays, empty kegs, broken suction cups, and bent blades. Nearby, a smaller memorial plaque honored Howard. The likeness captured the champion mid-blast, nose wrinkled, shield raised. At the unveiling, the statue let out a metallic “BWAAAP” and exhaled a puff of burnt-cheese smoke—clearly rigged, a crass, perfect tribute. Nobody laughed. They dropped to their knees, plugged their noses, and chanted, “BEANS FOREVER! BUTT BLASTER—KING OF FARTS!”

The plaque read: HOWARD COSSMELL — HE INHALED SO WE COULD CHEER. The league rolled on through a cosmos-wide haze of stink, like someone had farted history and forgotten to open a window.
